Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorat...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?rende...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/pla...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/v...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Primary: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ export const Disabled: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const InsideCard: Story = { │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}

import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

    12→  const location = useLocation()

    15→    window.scrollTo(0, 0)

    21→      <Routes>

    24→        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />

    27→        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
    28→        {children}
    29→      </Routes>
    30→    </>
    31→  )
    32→}

file.content:
import { DefaultTheme } from 'styled-components'

declare module 'styled-components' {
  export interface DefaultTheme {
    name: 'light' | 'dark'
    fonts: {
      family: string
    }
    spacing: typeof spacing
    color: typeof color
    borderRadius: typeof borderRadius
    boxShadow: typeof boxShadow
    typography: typeof typography
  }
}

const baseColors = {
  white: '#FFFFFF ',
  black: '#202020',
  red: {
    base: '#D70808',
    light1: '#E57373',
    light2: '#EF9A9A',
    dark1: '#C62828',
    dark2: '#B71C1C',
  },
  otherBlack: '#2C2C2C ',
  grey: {
    base: '#909090',
    light1: '#A6A6A6',
    light2: '#BCBCBC',
    light3: '#D2D2D2',
    light4: '#E9E9E9',
    light5: '#F5F6F7',
    light6: '#F9F9F9',
    dark1: '#797979',
    dark2: '#636363',
    dark3: '#4D4D4D',
    dark4: '#363636',
    dark5: '#2C2C2C',
    dark6: '#202020',
  },
  green: {
    base: '#E5F8BC',
    light1: '#E8F9C3',
    light2: '#EAF9C9',
    light3: '#EDFAD0',
    light4: '#EFFBD7',
    light5: '#F2FCDE',
    dark1: '#CEDFA9',
    dark2: '#B7C696',
    dark3: '#A0AE84',
    dark4: '#899571',
    dark5: '#737C5E',
  },
  blue: {
    base: '#B1DDE4',
    light1: '#B9E0E7',
    light2: '#C1E4E9',
    light3: '#C8E7EC',
    light4: '#D0EBEF',
    light5: '#D8EEF2',
    dark1: '#9FC7CD',
    dark2: '#8EB1B6',
    dark3: '#7C9BA0',
    dark4: '#6A8589',
    dark5: '#596f72',
  },
}

const spaceUnit = 1

const borderRadius = {
  xs: '4px',
  s: '8px',
  m: '16px',
  l: '24px',
  xl: '32px',
  xxl: '40px',
  round: '50%',
}

const spacing = {
  xxs: `${0.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xs: `${0.5 * spaceUnit}em`,
  s: `${spaceUnit}em`,
  m: `${1.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  l: `${2 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xl: `${3.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xxl: `${5.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
}

const boxShadow = {
  card: '0px 14px 26px 0px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08)',
  inner: 'inset 0 3px 0 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05)',
  outerBorder: `0 0 0 1px ${baseColors.blue.dark2}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.light5}`,
}

const typography = {
  fontSize: {
    body: '1.125rem',
    bodyS: '1rem',
    bodyXS: '0.9rem',
    bodyXXS: '0.72rem',
    heading1: '2.74rem',
    heading2: '2.19rem',
    heading3: '1.75rem',
    heading4: '1.4rem',
  },
  fontWeight: {
    black: '900',
    bold: '700',
    medium: '500',
    regular: '400',
  },
}

const color = {
  accentText: baseColors.black,
  badgeBackground: baseColors.grey.light4,
  badgeText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  cartButtonText: baseColors.grey.base,
  bannerBackground: baseColors.blue.base,
  buttonClear: 'transparent',
  buttonClearHover: baseColors.grey.light5,
  buttonPrimary: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  buttonPrimaryHover: baseColors.grey.dark5, // recheck
  buttonSecondary: baseColors.green.base,
  buttonSecondaryHover: baseColors.green.dark1, // recheck
  buttonText: baseColors.white,
  cardBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  checkoutTopBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  checkoutBottomBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  error: baseColors.red.dark2,
  formBackground: baseColors.white,
  foodItemBackground: baseColors.white,
  footerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
  headerBackground: baseColors.white,
  restaurantDetailBackground: baseColors.white,
  headerBorder: baseColors.grey.light4,
  inputBackground: baseColors.grey.light5,
  inputHint: baseColors.grey.dark1,
  inputIcon: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  label: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  labelActive: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  menuSectionBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  stepsIndicatorInnerBar: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  stepsIndicatorOuterBar: baseColors.grey.light5,
  orderSummaryBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayHeader: baseColors.grey.light5,
  primaryText: baseColors.otherBlack,
  secondaryText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  // This is just used to demo a11y violations, use it in the Review component
  bannerText: baseColors.blue.dark3,
  reviewText: baseColors.blue.dark5,
  screenBackground: baseColors.white,
  sidebarHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  sidebarFooter: baseColors.white,
  skeletonBase: baseColors.grey.light4,
  skeletonHighlight: baseColors.grey.light5,
  topBannerBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  newRestaurantTag: baseColors.green.base,
  white: baseColors.white,
  black: baseColors.black,
}

export const lightTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  borderRadius,
  boxShadow,
  color,
  fonts: {
    family: 'NunitoSans, sans-serif',
  },
  name: 'light',
  spacing,
  typography,
}

export const darkTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  ...lightTheme,
  boxShadow: {
    ...boxShadow,
    outerBorder: `0 0 0 2px ${baseColors.blue.dark5}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.base}`,
  },
  color: {
    ...lightTheme.color,
    badgeBackground: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    badgeText: baseColors.grey.light1,
    cartButtonText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
    bannerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    buttonClearHover: baseColors.grey.dark4, // recheck
    buttonPrimary: baseColors.green.base,
    buttonPrimaryHover: baseColors.green.dark1, // recheck
    buttonSecondary: baseColors.green.base,
    buttonText: baseColors.otherBlack,
    cardBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    checkoutTopBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    checkoutBottomBackground: baseColors.grey.dark1,
    formBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    foodItemBackground: baseColors.otherBlack,
    headerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    restaurantDetailBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    headerBorder: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    inputBackground: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    inputIcon: baseColors.white,
    inputHint: baseColors.grey.light1,
    label: baseColors.grey.light4,
    labelActive: baseColors.white,
    menuSectionBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    stepsIndicatorInnerBar: baseColors.green.base,
    stepsIndicatorOuterBar: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    orderSummaryBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    overlayBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    overlayHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    primaryText: baseColors.white,
    reviewText: baseColors.blue.base,
    screenBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    sidebarHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    sidebarFooter: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    skeletonBase: baseColors.grey.dark3,
    skeletonHighlight: baseColors.grey.dark2,
    topBannerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
  },
  name: 'dark',
}

    10→type SidebarProps = {
    11→  isOpen: boolean

    13→  onClose: () => void

    17→export const Sidebar: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<SidebarProps>> = ({
    18→  children,

    21→  title,

    61→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    62→      >

import styled from 'styled-components'

import { PageTemplate } from '../../templates/PageTemplate'
import { categories } from '../../stub/categories'

import { RestaurantsSection } from './components/RestaurantsSection'
import { CategoriesSection } from './components/CategoriesSection/CategoriesSection'
import { Banner } from './components/Banner'
import { AwardWinningSection } from './components/AwardWinningSection'

const Spacing = styled.div`
  margin-bottom: 4.5rem;
`

export const HomePage = () => (
  <PageTemplate>
    <Banner />
    <Spacing />
    <RestaurantsSection title="Our favorite picks" />
    <Spacing />
    <AwardWinningSection />
    <Spacing />
    <CategoriesSection categories={categories} />
    <Spacing />
  </PageTemplate>
)

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me now plan the implementation: ## Plan: ### 1. Update `.storybook/preview.tsx` The app needs: - `BrowserRouter` from react-router-dom - `Provider` (StoreProvider) from react-redux - `ThemeProvider` from styled-components - `GlobalStyle` component - `lightTheme` from styles/theme The `useDarkMode` hook uses localStorage, but since we're defaulting to light mode in storybook (false), we don't need to seed anything specifically. For the store, since each story should have a fresh store, we need to create a new store per story, or use a shared store. Looking at the app, the store is a singleton, so we can use the same store in Storybook since each story test will be isolated. ### 2. Create `.storybook/preview-body.html` Add `
` for the modal portal. ### 3. Write stories for these components: 1. `Button` - Primary/Clear/Disabled/WithIcon 2. `Badge` - various text values 3. `RestaurantCard` - Normal/ Loading/Closed/New 4. `Review` - various ratings 5. `Header` / `HeaderComponent` - Default/LogoOnly/WithCart 6. `TopBanner` - WithTitle/WithPhoto 7. `ShoppingCartMenu` - Open/Empty 8. `CategoryListPage` - page level 9. `HomePage` - page level 10. `CheckoutPage` - page level Now I'm thinking about how to verify CSS in the stories. Since the Button uses styled-components, I can't check static class names, but I can inspect the computed styles at runtime. The GlobalStyle sets the body background based on the theme, so for light mode that would be white, though I need to check what the Button itself applies for its background-color since that's also computed through styled-components. For the primary Button in light mode, the background color should be `rgb(32, 32, 32)`. Now I'm setting up the preview and story files—I need to decide whether to use the singleton store or create a fresh one per story. Since the store is exported as a singleton from the app state, I'll create a new Redux store in the decorator for each story using `configureStore` with the root reducer to keep things isolated. Setting up the decorator with fresh store and Provider... For routing, I'll use `MemoryRouter` instead of `BrowserRouter` to avoid issues with the actual browser environment in Storybook. The global decorator should use `MemoryRouter` with a default root path, but for page stories like `RestaurantDetailPage` that need `useParams`, I'll need to wrap them in a `Route` with the appropriate path configuration. Now I'm mapping out all the story files I need to create—the basic components like Button, Badge, and RestaurantCard, the presentational components like HeaderComponent and TopBanner, the container components like ShoppingCartMenu, and the three page stories. For the CssCheck story in Button, I'll showcase the primary button in light theme. Setting up the Storybook preview configuration... Now I'm checking whether `msw-storybook-addon` is installed—I can see `msw` is in the dependencies but not the addon itself, and I need to verify if the API mocking setup is complete. Actually, looking at the code, the app already has built-in mock detection through the `isMockedEnvironment` check in the API module, which automatically switches to `MockedRestaurantsApi` when running in Storybook. That means I don't need to set up MSW separately. The one thing I need to handle is the modal portal—since stories won't render the full `AppRoutes` component that creates the `#modal` div, I should add that element to `preview-body.html` so the Portal component has a target to render into.

1→import { useRef, useEffect } from 'react'
     2→import * as React from 'react'
     3→import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'
     4→
     5→type Props = {
     6→  selector: string
     7→}
     8→
     9→export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
    10→  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)
    11→
    12→  useEffect(() => {
    13→    const element = document.querySelector<Element>(selector)
    14→    if (element) {
    15→      ref.current = element
    16→    }
    17→  }, [selector])
    18→
    19→  return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null
    20→}
    21→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

Now I have enough information to write all the stories. Let me plan the stories and get to work: ## Plan of files to create/edit: 1. **Update `.storybook/preview.tsx`** - Add providers, GlobalStyle, ThemeProvider, MemoryRouter 2. **Create `.storybook/preview-body.html`** - Add `
` 3. **Create story files:** - `src/components/Button/Button.stories.tsx` - includes CssCheck - `src/components/Badge/Badge.stories.tsx` - `src/components/RestaurantCard/RestaurantCard.stories.tsx` - `src/components/Review/Review.stories.tsx` - `src/components/Header/Header.stories.tsx` - uses HeaderComponent - `src/components/TopBanner/TopBanner.stories.tsx` - `src/components/ShoppingCartMenu/ShoppingCartMenu.stories.tsx` - `src/pages/HomePage/HomePage.stories.tsx` - `src/pages/CategoryListPage/CategoryListPage.stories.tsx` - `src/pages/CheckoutPage/CheckoutPage.stories.tsx` The app uses BrowserRouter, so I'll need to handle routing in the preview setup. For the global decorator, I'll use MemoryRouter since it works well in Storybook, and I'll set up a fresh Redux store for each story using configureStore with the root reducer. I also need to wrap everything with ThemeProvider using the light theme and include GlobalStyle. For pages that depend on route parameters like RestaurantDetailPage, I'll handle those individually with Routes and specific route configurations. I need to wrap pages that need route params in their stories with proper routing setup. 1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}
   101→

content.thinking:
The `CategoryListPage.stories.tsx` failed on the `Default` story - it can't find the text "What's on the menu?". The error says "getByText" was looking for it in the DOM. 

Looking at the error output, there's some DOM content about categories (pizza, sushi etc.). The issue might be that the heading uses a different text rendering or there's an encoding issue with the apostrophe.

Let me check: `CategoryListPage.tsx` has:
```tsx
<StyledHeading level={2}>What's on the menu?</StyledHeading>


```

The apostrophe looks like a regular ASCII character in both the component and the test query, but the heading text isn't showing up in the DOM output - I'm only seeing the categories and TopBanner content, which suggests the heading might not be rendering at all or it's being hidden somehow.

```
Looking at the error more carefully, the DOM output shows the category cards are rendering but the "What's on the menu?" heading isn't being found by the test. This could mean the text is split across multiple DOM nodes, there's a character encoding issue with the quotation marks, or the heading is positioned outside the visible canvas area.
```

tsx
<StyledHeading level={2}>What's on the menu?</StyledHeading>
```

Both the component and the test use standard ASCII apostrophes, so they should match. The issue might be that `StyledHeading` is wrapping the text in a way that prevents `getByText` from finding it with exact matching, so I should try using a regex pattern instead. The `ShowsCategories` story test passes, which suggests the problem is specific to this component's structure.

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import styled, { css } from 'styled-components'
     3→
     4→type FontSize = 'heading1' | 'heading2' | 'heading3' | 'heading4'
     5→
     6→const HeadingBase = styled.h1<{ $level: number }>(
     7→  ({
     8→    $level,
     9→    theme: {
    10→      color,
    11→      typography: { fontSize },
    12→    },
    13→  }) => css`
    14→    margin: 0;
    15→    color: ${color.primaryText};
    16→    font-family: 'Montserrat';
    17→    letter-spacing: ${$level === 1 || $level === 2 ? '-2px' : 'unset'};
    18→    font-size: ${fontSize[`heading${$level}` as FontSize]};
    19→  `
    20→)
    21→
    22→type DefaultProps = {
    23→  level?: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5
    24→  className?: string
    25→}
    26→
    27→type HeadingProps = DefaultProps & Omit<React.ComponentProps<'h1'>, keyof DefaultProps>
    28→
    29→export const Heading: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<HeadingProps>> = ({
    30→  level = 1,
    31→  children,
    32→  className,
    33→}) => {
    34→  const heading = `h${level}` as React.ElementType
    35→  return (
    36→    <HeadingBase as={heading} $level={level} className={className}>
    37→      {children}
    38→    </HeadingBase>
    39→  )
    40→}

The `Heading` component renders as an `h{level}` tag. So with `level={2}`, it renders as `

What's on the menu?

`. The `getByText("What's on the menu?")` should find it. But wait - looking at the test error again. The error says the element was NOT found. But actually looking at the DOM snippet in the error, it only showed a portion of the DOM. Maybe the heading IS in the DOM but the error display was just showing nearby elements. Let me try a different approach - use `getByRole('heading', { name: /what's on the menu/i })` which would be more semantic. `M-bM-^@M-^Y` is the UTF-8 encoding of the right single quotation mark (Unicode `U+2019` = `'`). So the source file uses a curly apostrophe, not a straight ASCII apostrophe. I need to update my test to use the right character or use a regex.

content.text:
All 39 tests pass across 13 story files, and TypeScript checks out cleanly. Here's a summary of what was set up:

**`.storybook/preview.tsx`** — Updated with:
- `MemoryRouter` for routing
- Fresh Redux `Provider` (new store per story)
- `ThemeProvider` with `lightTheme`
- `GlobalStyle` for styled-components global CSS
- MSW via `msw-storybook-addon` to intercept API requests

**`.storybook/preview-body.html`** — Created with `<div id="modal">` for the portal root used by modals

**`.storybook/main.ts`** — Added `staticDirs: ['../public']` to serve `mockServiceWorker.js`

**`.storybook/msw-handlers.ts`** — MSW handlers for the restaurants API (all, by id, by category)

**10 story files** (all passing with `ai-generated` tag and play functions):
1. `Button.stories.tsx` — Primary, Clear, Disabled, Large, WithIcon, **CssCheck** (verifies `rgb(32, 32, 32)` background)
2. `Badge.stories.tsx` — Default, Pizza, ComfortFood
3. `RestaurantCard.stories.tsx` — Default, Closed, New, Loading
4. `Review.stories.tsx` — NoRating, VeryGood, Excellent
5. `Header.stories.tsx` — Default, LogoOnly, WithCartItems
6. `TopBanner.stories.tsx` — WithTitle, WithPhoto, NoTitle
7. `ShoppingCartMenu.stories.tsx` — Open, Empty, Closed
8. `HomePage.stories.tsx` — Default, WithRestaurants (waits for MSW data)
9. `CategoryListPage.stories.tsx` — Default, ShowsCategories
10. `CheckoutPage.stories.tsx` — Default, WithForm

providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Primary: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ export const Disabled: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const InsideCard: Story = { │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

`createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Primary: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ export const Disabled: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const InsideCard: Story = { │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}

    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {

    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

Main Entry Files **src/index.tsx** - React DOM entry point - Uses `React.StrictMode` - Mounts the app to `#root` element using `createRoot()` - Registers service worker as unregistered (disabled offline support) **src/App.tsx** - Root application component ```typescript Providers (in order): 1. BrowserRouter (React Router) 2. StoreProvider (Redux with store) 3. ThemeProvider (styled-components) 4. GlobalStyle component Features: - Dark mode toggle using 'use-dark-mode' hook - Dynamically switches between lightTheme and darkTheme - Renders AppRoutes component ``` ### 2. Provider Components & State Management **Redux Store** (src/app-state/): - **Store**: `/src/app-state/store.ts` - Configured with Redux Toolkit - Combines two reducers: cart and order - Exports: `store`, `AppDispatch`, `RootState` - **Cart Slice** (`/src/app-state/cart/cart.ts`) - State: `{ visible: boolean, items: CartItem[] }` - Actions: `toggleVisibilityAction`, `saveItemAction`, `clearItemAction`, `removeItemAction`, `clearCartAction` - **Order Slice** (`/src/app-state/order/order.ts`) - State: `{ items: CartItem[] }` - Actions: `saveOrderAction`, `clearOrderAction` **Custom Hooks** (`/src/app-state/hooks.ts`): - `useAppDispatch()` - Typed dispatch hook - `useAppSelector()` - Typed selector hook with RootState **Other Providers**: - **React Router** - BrowserRouter wraps the application - **styled-components ThemeProvider** - Switches between light/dark themes - **use-dark-mode** - Manages dark mode state (uses localStorage internally) ### 3. CSS Files Imported at Root **Imported in GlobalStyle.tsx** (`/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx`): 1. `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css` - Carousel component styles 2. `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css` - Skeleton loading styles **Stories CSS** (imported in story files): 1. `/src/stories/button.css` - Button story styles 2. `/src/stories/header.css` - Header story styles 3. `/src/stories/page.css` - Page story styles **Styled-Components Usage**: - All component styling uses styled-components instead of CSS files - GlobalStyle defines custom fonts (Montserrat, Hind), container classes, and carousel overrides ### 4. Fetch/Network Calls & API Integration **API Module** (`/src/api/`): **Main API** (`/src/api/index.ts`): - Base URL: `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants` - Uses axios for HTTP requests - Implements client-side caching (Map-based) - Disables caching in test/Storybook environments **API Methods**: - `getRestaurants()` - Fetches all restaurants - `getRestaurantById(id: string)` - Fetches single restaurant by ID - `getRestaurantsByCategory(category: string)` - Fetches restaurants by category **Conditional Logic API** (`/src/api/conditional-logic.ts`): - Provides mock implementation (`MockedRestaurantsApi`) for Storybook/test environments - Returns mock data from `/src/stub/restaurants.ts` - Dynamically switches between real and mocked APIs based on environment **Network Hooks** (`/src/api/hooks.ts`): 1. `useFetchRestaurants()` - Returns `{ status, restaurants, retryRequest }` 2. `useFetchRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)` - Returns `{ status, restaurants, retryRequest }` 3. `useFetchRestaurant(id)` - Returns `{ status, restaurant, retryRequest }` All hooks implement: - Loading state management - Error handling (status codes) - Retry mechanism - Dependencies on id/categoryId parameters ### 5. localStorage/sessionStorage/Cookie Usage **use-dark-mode Library**: - `useDarkMode()` hook in `/src/App.tsx` and `/src/components/Header/Header.tsx` - Automatically manages dark mode preference in localStorage - Syncs across browser tabs - No explicit localStorage/sessionStorage/document.cookie reads in application code **No direct storage access found** in the codebase - all storage is managed by the use-dark-mode library. ### 6. ` as portal root in Routes component **Portal Root in HTML** (`/index.html`): - Defines `
` in body (line 32) **Modal Usage** (`/src/components/Modal/Modal.tsx`): - Uses Portal with selector `#modal` - Implements CSSTransition animations for modal and backdrop - Keyboard support (Escape to close) - Body scroll lock when open ### 7.      4→
     5→type Props = {
     6→  selector: string
     7→}

     9→export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
    10→  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)
    11→
    12→  useEffect(() => {
    13→    const element = document.querySelector<Element>(selector)
    14→    if (element) {
    15→      ref.current = element

    20→}
